data.title: PG&E: An Undervalued Utility
data.description:
PG&E Corp. (PCG) is a $35 billion utility unit that has been consistently punished by PG&E's history of mismanagement and wildfires. Despite that, the company is working hard to reorient itself. That is positioning the company well for consistent EPS growth and, as we'll see throughout this article, will enable strong shareholder returns for those who invest now.

PG&E Growth

PG&E had $0.50 in non-GAAP core EPS in 3Q 2025, or $1.14 YTD, on track for the company.

PG&E has narrowed its guidance to $1.50 in EPS for the year, putting the company at a P/E of ~10.5x. That's incredibly low for a utility company, especially one that's targeting 9+% in earnings growth for the next 5 years. PG&E is continuing to work with the government to continue its massive capital buildout ($73 billion) with no new equity through 2030.

Additionally, the company is targeting hitting a 20% dividend payout ratio by 2028. For perspective, the company's targeted 2030 EPS is $2.31/share, which implies minimum dividends of $0.46 based on PG&E's payout policy. That's a strong but cheap 3% dividend yield that the company can comfortably afford with a P/E of just over 6x based on your cost basis today.

PG&E Wildfire Policy

The biggest question for PG&E is wildfires as it works to avoid what could be a liability in the $10s of billions.

California is now taking a full state-level approach to handling wildfire risk. From PG&E, that's $10s of billions of capital aiming to solve all incidents and structures. The company has lost no structures in 2023-2025, with 2022 also a substantial improvement. The company has continued to clear vegetation and underground its program along with continuous monitoring.

The California Wildfire Fund was impacted by the early-2025 LA wildfires, but the fund is set up to have an $18 billion continuation account. That will help prevent these utilities from being destroyed by wildfires that increase as climate change becomes worse.

PG&E Data Center

At the same time, PG&E is deploying assets to take advantage of data center demand.

Every GW of data center pipeline is expected to result in a 1-2% electric bill reduction. With almost 10 GWs in the pipeline, that indicates a ~15% electric bill reduction at the midpoint. Only a small portion of this is under construction; however, 18 projects totaling 1.6 GW are under final engineering, which increased by 100 MWs in the last quarter.

While California doesn't have the masses of cheap electricity of other states that will make it data center central, in our view, the utility has enough to focus on right now. East Coast grids are seeing potential massive customer costs from data center assets that have yet to be built, and PG&E needs to focus on being cost competitive to its current customers.

PG&E Investments

PG&E's massive capital program is visible below.

The company is planning on deploying $73 billion in capital over the next 5 years the vast majority in 2028-2030F. PG&E expects to ramp up its rate base as a result of these investments, a substantial percentage of which is already authorized by PG&E. These are one-time mega investments in PG&E's grid that will provide long-term returns.

PG&E is doing this while continuing to invest massively in renewables and electrical storage. In 2023 PG&E gave retail customers 100% carbon-free electricity, and that positions the company well for the long term as it continues to target carbon neutrality for 2040.

PG&E Shareholder Returns

Putting all of these investments together, PG&E is well positioned to generate strong cash flow.

The company is focused on ending equity issuance and focusing on rate base growth and strong returns. It expects load growth to be ~2% annualized and customer bills to be ~3% annualized in line with inflation. We'd like the company to focus on this customer bill component; it has minimal goodwill after the wildfires, and maintaining several years of flat bills would go far.

The company's 2025F dividend payout ratio is only 7%, but operating cash flow is $9 billion with 10% non-GAAP core EPS growth. That's supposed to switch to the high single digits through 2030 as shareholder returns remain high.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is PG&E's ability to continue executing. PG&E has had to deal with some of the highest bills in the country and massive investments needed to mitigate wildfire risk. The company has a plan in place, but it needs years of execution to hit the point where it can drive valuable shareholder returns.

Conclusion

PG&E has an impressive portfolio of assets, but its market capitalization has been crushed to roughly $35 billion. That's because of the multi-year impact of wildfires, with the company ~75% below its 2017 highs. However, PG&E has a plan to underground transmission lines, and already initial work is paying off in risks.

Going forward, PG&E plans to continue expanding its rate base, with the potential to benefit from data centers. PG&E is investing tens of billions, which it expects will lead to lofty shareholder returns. The company has almost $60 billion of debt, which will expand with this capital program, but PG&E will be able to use it to drive strong dividends and shareholder returns.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.
